title: Listening - Part 4: Presenting Community Radio
description: You will hear part of a radio interview with a woman called Priya, who presents community radio programmes. For questions 24–30, choose the correct answer (A, B or C).

content.audio_script: Presenter: Today I’m speaking to Priya Shah, who presents a weekly programme on community radio. Priya, were you always confident about speaking on air? || Priya: Absolutely not. At university I avoided giving presentations whenever possible. Then a community station advertised for volunteers to help research local stories. [24]I applied because the role was supposed to be behind the scenes, and only later discovered that I enjoyed speaking into a microphone[/24]. It was a very unexpected career direction. || Presenter: What was your first programme about? || Priya: A market in our town was facing closure, and I interviewed stallholders and shoppers. I was worried it would sound amateur, but people listened. [25]Several listeners contacted the council after hearing the programme, and the market was eventually given more time to find a solution[/25]. That showed me radio could have a practical impact locally. || Presenter: Is interviewing people easy? || Priya: It becomes easier with practice, but preparing questions is only part of it. Beginners sometimes concentrate so hard on their list that they miss the interesting thing a guest has just said. [26]The skill I had to develop was listening closely enough to ask a useful follow-up question rather than simply moving on[/26]. That makes a conversation sound natural. || Presenter: How has technology changed community radio? || Priya: We still broadcast at set times, but all programmes are also available online. This has made a big difference to our audience. [27]People who moved away from the town now listen to our podcasts because they want to keep in touch with local events[/27]. In a way, local radio has become international. || Presenter: What happens when you make a mistake live on air? || Priya: Early on, I feared every silence or wrong word. Once I announced the wrong date for a festival and felt awful. [28]I corrected the information immediately and learned that listeners are usually forgiving when you are honest about an error[/28]. Panicking only makes a small mistake seem bigger. || Presenter: You also train teenage volunteers. What surprises them? || Priya: They expect the exciting part to be presenting, but broadcasting requires plenty of quiet work. [29]They are often amazed by how long it takes to check facts and obtain permission before a short interview can be used[/29]. Responsible radio cannot just repeat a rumour because it sounds entertaining. || Presenter: What sort of person would suit community radio? || Priya: Not necessarily somebody with a dramatic voice. [30]Being genuinely interested in other people’s experiences is more valuable than wanting everyone to notice you[/30]. A good presenter gives local people room to tell their own stories.

task.title: Part 4
task.instruction: For questions 24–30, choose the correct answer (A, B or C).

questions.question: How did Priya first become involved in radio?

options.A: She wanted to improve her confidence in public.
options.B: She expected to work away from the microphone.
options.C: She had already presented programmes at university.

questions.explanation: Priya applied for a behind-the-scenes research role and only later found she liked speaking on air.

questions.question: Why was Priya's first programme important to her?

options.A: It helped bring about action in the community.
options.B: It led to an offer of paid radio work.
options.C: It attracted more listeners than national programmes.

questions.explanation: Listeners contacted the council after the programme and the threatened market gained more time.

questions.question: What interviewing skill did Priya need to develop?

options.A: Preparing a shorter list of questions.
options.B: Helping nervous guests speak more slowly.
options.C: Responding properly to what guests say.

questions.explanation: She learned to listen closely and ask useful follow-up questions.

questions.question: What does Priya say about online programmes?

options.A: They attract former residents who live elsewhere.
options.B: They are mainly listened to by younger people.
options.C: They have reduced interest in scheduled broadcasts.

questions.explanation: She says former local residents use podcasts to keep in touch with events in the town.

questions.question: What did Priya learn after giving incorrect information on air?

options.B: Listeners accept errors if they are corrected honestly.
options.C: Live broadcasting is less reliable than recorded shows.

questions.explanation: She corrected the date straight away and found listeners forgiving when she admitted an error.

questions.question: What surprises teenage radio volunteers?

options.A: The time involved in verifying and approving material.
options.B: The difficulty of finding enjoyable local stories.
options.C: The number of people needed for each broadcast.

questions.explanation: Priya says they are amazed by the time required to check facts and get permission.

questions.question: According to Priya, what quality is most important for a presenter?

options.A: a voice which attracts people's attention
options.B: an interest in what other people have to say
options.C: the confidence to express strong opinions

questions.explanation: She says real interest in other people's experiences matters more than seeking attention.
